skill.name: coding-with-tailiwnd
skill.description: Guides component styling with Tailwind CSS directives, layered component classes, and Tailwind 4.1 reference files.
skill.summary: Create Tailwind CSS components with structured component styles and local Tailwind 4.1 guidance.
skill.author: Emz1998

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported external command, weak cryptography, and network reconnaissance patterns, but the cited text is Markdown inline code, Tailwind terms, and local documentation paths. No executable code, prompt injection, network access, credential access, or malicious intent was found in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: Dismissed false positive: Markdown inline Tailwind directives
low_findings.description: The static analyzer treated Markdown backticks around Tailwind directives as Ruby or shell backtick execution. These lines describe Tailwind CSS syntax and do not execute commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious backticks are Markdown formatting around CSS directives. There is no shell, Ruby, script file, or command invocation context.

low_findings.title: Dismissed false positive: local documentation and component paths
low_findings.description: The cited paths are local reference files and component directory guidance. They are not shell commands, network targets, or data exfiltration paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are written as file path references inside instructions. They do not include command separators, interpreters, URLs, or dynamic input.

low_findings.title: Dismissed false positive: weak cryptography and reconnaissance keywords
low_findings.description: The reported high-risk crypto and reconnaissance matches appear to be keyword collisions in normal Tailwind CSS description text and a Markdown file name. No cryptographic operation or network scan is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain descriptive prose and a local Markdown path. I found no hash function call, encryption code, host discovery, port scanning, or network request.

content.value_statement: Teams need consistent Tailwind CSS component styling without mixing every rule into markup. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to use layered CSS files, Tailwind directives, and local Tailwind 4.1 references.
actual_capabilities: Directs the assistant to create separate CSS files for component styling.
Promotes Tailwind apply usage for reusable component classes.
Uses Tailwind layer components guidance for parent and child selectors.
Points to local Tailwind 4.1 documentation files when they exist.
Encourages custom utilities and variants through Tailwind directives.
Defines component placement rules for the project component directory.
limitations: The skill does not include the referenced Tailwind 4.1 documentation files.
It does not validate whether the project uses Tailwind 4.1.
The component directory is written as srs/components and may require project confirmation.
It provides workflow rules, not complete component templates.

use_cases.title: Create a styled feature component
use_cases.description: Build a new frontend component with a separate CSS file and Tailwind component-layer styles.

use_cases.title: Standardize component CSS
use_cases.description: Move repeated Tailwind utility combinations into reusable component classes for a cleaner codebase.

use_cases.title: Apply Tailwind 4.1 project guidance
use_cases.description: Use available local Tailwind reference files before changing theme variables, directives, utilities, or variants.

prompt_templates.title: Style a simple component
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Tailwind CSS component for a user profile summary. Use a separate CSS file and follow the component layer rules from this skill.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor repeated utilities
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor this component so repeated Tailwind utility groups become reusable component classes. Keep behavior unchanged and explain the styling changes.

prompt_templates.title: Add custom variants
prompt_templates.prompt: Add custom Tailwind utilities and variants for this component. Check local Tailwind 4.1 guidance first if those files exist.

prompt_templates.title: Build a component system pattern
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a reusable card component pattern with parent and child classes in the component layer. Avoid editing the UI component directory.

output_examples.input: Create a notification banner with reusable Tailwind styling.
output: A new notification banner component is described with a separate CSS file.
The styles use a component layer and reusable class names.
The assistant notes any local Tailwind reference files it used.

output_examples.input: Clean up a component that has repeated utility classes.
output: Repeated spacing, border, and state styles are grouped into named component classes.
The component markup becomes easier to scan.
No protected UI component directory files are changed.

best_practices: Confirm the actual component directory before creating files.
Use local Tailwind 4.1 reference files when they are present.
Keep component-layer classes focused on reusable visual patterns.
anti_patterns: Do not edit protected UI component directory files unless the project owner asks for it.
Do not assume Tailwind 4.1 features exist without checking the project setup.
Do not move every one-off utility into a component class.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps create and refactor Tailwind CSS components using separate CSS files and component-layer styling rules.

faq.question: Does it generate complete components by itself?
faq.answer: It provides styling workflow guidance. The assistant still needs the project context and requested component behavior.

faq.question: Does it require Tailwind 4.1?
faq.answer: It references Tailwind 4.1 guidance, but it does not verify the installed Tailwind version.

faq.question: Can it edit existing UI library files?
faq.answer: The skill instructs the assistant not to touch the protected UI component directory.

faq.question: Why use a separate CSS file?
faq.answer: A separate CSS file can keep repeated component styles organized and easier to reuse.

faq.question: Are the referenced Tailwind documentation files included?
faq.answer: No. The skill points to local project files that must already exist in the workspace.
